data.title: Roblox's Platform Is Maturing Faster Than Wall Street Thinks
data.description:
I have been keeping an eye on Roblox (NYSE:RBLX) for a while now. The company is still perceived as a kid’s game, which I believe is too reductive. The reality seems to be much different, with the platform becoming more like an infrastructure for social gaming and virtual economies. The most recent financial results seem to prove that a shift is underway. Let’s dissect this.

Booking growth and monetization optionality

In 2Q25, the bookings were $1.44B, something not seen many times in companies this size, representing a massive year-over-year growth of 51%, while the DAU growth was 41%, this being the main indicator that the monetization is expanding. Bear in mind that the bookings are the true demand signal, representing the cash received for Robux and other subscriptions. The fact that bookings are growing faster than DAU means that this is a monetization unlock story.

The FCF metrics support our assertion that the monetization is being unlocked. We can dig deeper into a more granular level to unpack a couple of dynamics that are tailwinds to monetization. For starters, the virtual economy with in-app Robux purchases is increasing ARPPU. The ad-based monetization in rewarded video is integrated with Google’s ads and is scaling fast. On another note, creators can now license their IP, a bit like other creator-brand collaborations on YouTube. Finally, AI tools are now available, allowing for rapid goods creation, thus reducing time to market.

Global expansion as a growth engine

What most people aren’t paying attention to is how fast they are scaling on the international front, and the upside this can unleash in the next 2 to 3 years. The company’s business model is heavily dependent on getting people on the platform and keeping them engaged to eventually drive some form of monetization. Basically, international users are going through the same funnel as the US, just maybe 2 or 3 years behind.

A couple of recent improvements are having a huge impact on driving international growth. The burst of data centers capacity in Asia is helping to get lower latency, and auto translation is unlocking the potential in countries like Japan.

Monetization efficiency and creator flywheel

One of the most overlooked metrics is average bookings per daily user growth. This metric is growing at 20% year over year. Basically, this tells us that users are spending more. However, developers are also gaining more, with developers’ payouts now growing at 52%. That means that developers can make real money in the platform, which creates a virtuous cycle where better monetization attracts more creators, making better games, which should improve monetization.

Valuation

To give a proper look at valuation, we need to select a peer set that captures the many dimensions of the platform, and to be honest, there isn’t a completely direct comparable. In my opinion, the closest comparables are Unity (U), Duolingo (DUOL), Snap (SNAP), Meta (META), and YouTube (Google) (GOOG)(GOOGL). Now, the problems begin because when you look at the valuation for Roblox and peers, we get the sense that it is insane.

If you look at the table, the company is trading at 97x price to FCF and 59x EV to FCF. I agree, this looks crazy. However, there are a few key reasons. First, FCF just inflected. The company went from burning cash to generating cash, and its FCF margins are still expanding. Therefore, this is a work in progress. On the other hand, bookings growth is at 51% YoY, while FCF is lagging this metric because of deferred revenue accounting. Therefore, we can expect the valuation to re-anchor if the company keeps hitting the high end of the guidance. And this is not far-fetched, since the company is now monetizing a very sticky user base.

Risks

One of the major risks the platform has been exposed to is predator concerns. As I keep following the company, these issues keep popping up periodically, making it almost feel like a time bomb. Even with a big effort in deploying trust and safety mechanisms, we are still hit with periodic media firestorms. And this is also likely to attract regulators’ attention. And make no mistake, more regulations will mean slower growth.

Another issue that I keep seeing mentioned by analysts is the over-reliance on a few big hits. To thrive, the whole ecosystem depends on having a mid-tier segment of games that is visible enough to keep the developers engaged in creating games. If only a couple of top hits end up getting most of the rewards, the ecosystem might dry up fast. On the other hand, one of the most interesting features that creates ecosystem momentum is user-generated content. The problem is that with UGC growth also comes the possibility of IP infringements. In the end, it is unlikely that lawsuits on this might destroy the company, but it is likely that it will divert resources, and it might cool down the ecosystem momentum.

The ad monetization is still a big uncertainty. Yes, they already have ad initiatives like Rewarded Video, but these initiatives are still in beta. This is not yet a meaningful revenue stream. The market seems to be already baking in some of that optionality, and if the results disappoint, the stock price will likely take a hit.

Finally, the high valuation leaves no room for error, meaning that any miss on the metrics we have covered (DAU, ABPDAU, FCF) might lead to a painful re-rating.

Investment Action

The company is trading at very rich multiples, and that makes it not for the faint of heart. However, if you zoom out, you can see that this is not a kids’ game anymore. We can distill the company’s ethos as a global platform for interactive entertainment. A kind of YouTube meets Fortnite.

The company is now reaping the benefits from developing a great discovery engine and creator tools that have unleashed a thriving economy and are now allowing the platform to break out organically. Yes, there are legitimate risks in terms of content moderation and high valuation, but at this pace, it is likely that the market will reward the company with an even higher market cap.

The revenue estimates show exactly that. This is a company with a lot baked in, but if the growth materializes, and the current platform performance seems to support this, then in a few years, the current valuation won’t look that expensive.

data.title: FSK KKR Capital: A Dividend Cut May Be Closer Than I Anticipated
data.description:
Introduction

With a weaker jobs report, it's apparent that we are likely to see multiple rate cuts by the end of the year.

As a result, the BDC (BIZD) sector is likely to face further selling pressure as dividend coverage becomes a focal point for many.

One BDC in particular, FSK KKR Capital Corp. (NYSE:FSK) has faced their fair share of headwinds since the rate hike cycle began.

However, the BDC had seemingly gotten these under control with improvements until their most recent quarter, where non-performing loans rose due to distressed borrowers.

With us expected to see multiple rate cuts by the end of 2025, investors should remain cautious about FSK's dividend sustainability, as I could see a cut before the year is up, or by the first quarter of 2026.

In this article, I discuss FSK's latest earnings, fundamentals, and why a dividend cut may be closer than I previously anticipated.

Previous Thesis

I last covered FSK KKR Capital back in July in an article titled: A Risky Yield, But I Think The Dividend Remains Safe for 2025.

Although I cautioned investors about FSK's yield and headwinds from lower rates, their improved credit quality, robust liquidity, and higher dividend income gave me confidence in their ability to sustain their dividend, at least for the near-term. Since, the stock is down double-digits while the S&P (SP500) is up over 6%.

Deteriorating Financials

FSK KKR Capital reported their second quarter earnings and experienced a decline in their financials. Net investment income fell $0.05 from the prior quarter to $0.62. On an adjusted basis, this also fell $0.05, from $0.65 to $0.60.

Their top line was down $2 million over the same period to $298 million. This was a result of lower interest income from a rise in non-accruals and lower fee income.

During the quarter, FSK successfully removed two companies from the list, but this was offset by the addition of 4 borrowers to non-accrual status. This is a result of tariff impacts & softer consumer demand. Unfortunately, 3 of these are FSK's larger borrowers.

But on a positive note, management stated that their estimated tariff impact has fallen modestly from 8% to the low to mid-single digit range. As expected, these realized losses resulted in a sizable decline in FSK's NAV.

This fell $1.44 quarter-over-quarter to $21.93. Year-over-year, NAV fell 8.3%. For comparison, the chart below shows FSK's NAV performance vs some of their larger peers in the sector.

Of the group, FSK KKR Capital and Blue Owl Capital Corp (OBDC) were the only two that saw NAV erosion, while Ares Capital (ARCC) saw growth a little higher than 1%, and Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL) saw roughly a half percent.

Typically, BDCs that experience consistent or high NAV erosion usually see their prices underperform.

Credit Quality

During my previous article, I commended FSK's improvements, especially from 2023, which saw non-accruals rise to an alarming rate. On a cost basis, these rose as high as 8.9%.

In Q1, non-accruals managed to decline to 3.5% on a cost basis and 2.1% on a fair value basis, improving from the previous year's quarter's 6.5% and 4.2% respectively.

But as previously mentioned, these rose to 5.3% and 3.0%, disappointing considering how management worked to restructure borrowers the last two years.

Slower M&A activity has also impacted the sector, down close to 10% from the prior year, a testament to the macro uncertainty we're facing currently.

In the quarter, management originated $1.4 billion of investments, bringing the year-to-date total to $3.4 billion. Most of Q2's investments were add-ons. New originations were mostly first-lien at 83%, bringing FSK's first-lien exposure total to 59%.

How Long Can They Sustain The Dividend?

As mentioned in my previous article, due to FSK's robust liquidity including spillover, revolver capacity, and non-accrual improvements, I suspected they would at least be able to keep the dividend safe for the remainder of the year.

And while management re-emphasized their plan during earnings, investors should remain cautious as dividend sustainability has dwindled.

With the decline in NII, this failed to cover the base dividend of $0.64, giving them coverage of 97%. Including the $0.06 supplemental, this was even lower.

Management stated they still plan to distribute $2.80 in total dividends, including $2.56 in base dividends and $0.24 in supplementals.

For comparison, despite their declines in their bottom lines, ARCC & BXSL still managed to cover their dividends with coverages of 102% and 100%, respectively.

Moreover, leverage also increased during the quarter to 1.20x from 1.14x, slightly above the peer average of 1.19x. While BDCs can use leverage for dividend sustainability temporarily, this gives FSK little flexibility financially.

Additionally, they have more than $1 billion in debt due next year. And despite upsizing their revolver capacity and having $3.1 billion in liquidity, FSK's dividend sustainability is at higher risk with the rise in non-accruals and leverage.

Moreover, the recent jobs report and downward revisions could be indications of the economy weakening further.

If so, FSK could see more rises in non-accruals in the coming quarters, something investors should also keep a close eye on.

Although M&A could improve in the coming quarters, lower base rates will provide further headwinds, making a dividend cut in the near-term likely.

Discount Valuation

Although FSK's dividend yield and P/NAV ratio are attractive at 16% and 0.77x, investors should remain cautious, as a distribution cut could send the share price falling further.

Furthermore, I expect the entire sector to experience weakness as a result of rate cuts in the near future. Investors looking for yields should at least wait until the beginning of 2026 to see how additional rate cuts flow through to BDC bottom lines.

Conclusion

Although FSK's management stated they remain committed to their distributions for this year, rising non-accruals, the potential to see further economic weakening, and lower base rates have increased my worries about their dividend sustainability for 2025.

While they can eliminate the supplemental and increase leverage, the base dividend coverage below 100%, their upcoming debt, and high leverage give them little flexibility. Despite me continuing to rate them a hold, I think a dividend cut before the end of this year is more likely now.

data.title: HHIS:CA Hits The Market Running
data.description:
The Harvest Diversified High Income Shares ETF (TSX:HHIS:CA) promises to be an interesting strategy that could live up to its high-income and low volatility proposition. There is not enough data to empirically dissect its performance, and that remains an important shortcoming in building a thesis, given some of the methodology elements are active (and for active strategies, the performance is also a core part of the thesis). But there are enough cues from similar methodologies to be able to take a call on what to expect.

Methodology

HHIS:CA is set up like an alternative mutual fund that allows greater investment flexibility. HHIS:CA is allowed to use leverage, which the methodology caps at 33%. This is an aggregate limit that accounts for leverage of the holdings as well as direct leverage. Since HHIS:CA selects up to 15 exchange traded mutual funds in its portfolio that employ covered call strategies on single equity securities, the leverage could come by means of an underlying ETF that is already leveraged or from HHIS:CA's leveraged position on the underlying or both.

The methodology does not specify any strict rebalancing period, which appears like an active call by the portfolio manager. However, any aggregate leverage breach of the 33% level triggers a rebalance aimed at bringing it down below the targeted threshold within 2 business days. The exact quantum of leverage is also dynamic and another active element in the methodology, meaning there could be times with no leverage at all and other times where the leverage is anywhere up to the 33% threshold.

Allocations are also an active management component from selection to weightings. The alternative mutual fund setup can imply significant concentration on single issuers as well. Cash holdings are maintained for operational purposes, and there is no defined framework mentioned in the methodology.

The underlying holdings are ETFs themselves, with HHIS:CA working as a fund of funds. These ETFs work on an income-generating option call writing philosophy where, again, depending on the choice of ETFs, an indirect active element is added on how much the options cover the underlying equity position and how far they are placed from the at the money strike.

Starting with Negative Bias

I have reviewed multiple call selling option strategies, like the underlying core of HHIS:CA and my views are detailed here. For the benefit of new readers, the summary is that such strategies have pitfalls beyond the known issues with their upside-capping shortcomings. What is less visible is that what seems like a relatively lower drawdown is still only less by a fixed margin (equal to the premiums earned), while the upside opportunity loss is not fixed. Over a long term, it misses more than it earns in most cases.

Then there are issues with aggressive yields that are not easily funded by the option layer alone. A significant chunk of the payout is funded by capital appreciation of the underlying, which does not have good room to grow.

Leverage and Diversification May Help

Having summarized the concerns around the option writing strategy as a generic instrument to generate income, it is not surprising that a vast majority of such strategies do pay out aggressively but lose NAV over time. The net result is a total return profile that does not grow well or does little to improve the volatility profile of owning the underlying directly.

There are exceptions, though. In my past analyses, I have seen such exceptions come from a handful of single-stock-based strategies where the volatility profile is conducive. An optimum volatility profile offers high premiums to be earned more often than not, yet is not so volatile that capturing the premium becomes difficult. Option-based income ETFs based on broader portfolios or including leverage are far fewer in number , but I have observed a greater proportion of such setups to do well. Which is why I find HHIS:CA, which combines both the factors, interesting!

Why am I excited about the leverage? Let's take a look at QQCL:CA, which I found to be a rare buy within the option-writing universe of ETFs. The main mechanism that makes QQCL:CA work is the combination of a dynamic call writing coverage limited to 50% of the portfolio's notional value and a 25% leverage. The leverage ensures more premiums earned and more upside capture, assuming the active management reduces the leverage on drawdowns. This has worked out well for QQCL:CA historically. The unleveraged version of QQCL:CA, QQCC:CA, is even better from a risk-adjusted return perspective, though. I have rated QQCC:CA a Strong Buy because it does not sacrifice the upside potential too much while mitigating risks better. Both the strategies outshine QYLD, like straightforward option writing strategies that are unleveraged and write options on a greater part of the portfolio. The more effective lever is the lower proportion of call coverage, but leverage too helps.

Let us check whether HHIS:CA also benefits from these two mechanisms. The portfolio has 15 components, so concentration risk is a given. The current setup does not show any material disproportionate allocation or deviation from an average 6.67% expected allocation if the portfolio was equally weighted, but the methodology does allow active calls that could make the portfolio far more concentrated than it is even now.

More importantly, I scanned all the holdings and saw each of the current holdings have an implied leverage of 25% and retain the 50% cap on notional written call coverage. HHIS:CA is only allowed a little more leverage room on top of this, but can be expected to be broadly in line with QQCL:CA in leverage and capped option writing terms.

Then there is another important matter of the underlying. Both QQCL:CA and QQCC:CA write options on a single underlying, which is already a broad, diversified NDX index. As noted before, single stocks often are not diversified enough to render a smoother risk-return profile to the income strategy. This is a reason why YMAX is working out well. It combines a group of underlying option writing ETFs like HHIS:CA, and mixes a bunch of large and smaller market cap names, ensuring the sub-components are either too big to ignore or come with significant trading interests (hence decent volatility levels). This mix of volatility profiles is key because that shows its performance differences in YMAX, whereas a large-cap Magnificent 7-focused YMAG falls behind. Even a lesser focus on stable, lower volatility large caps does not work out well, as seen in the case of ULTY.

Does HHIS:CA meet a YMAX-like balanced mix of underlying? I am slightly taken aback in here. While HHIS:CA does show a promising leverage and upside capturing profile, it falls short on the balance of the underlying I see in YMAX (or the NDX). HHIS:CA almost entirely focuses on large caps. I don't see sufficient smaller cap or higher volatility presence like Robinhood, Carvana, Gold Miners and Innovation Baskets. There are only one or two relatively higher volatility names like MSTR and Coinbase that look like they are providing a YMAX-like flavor, but the balance is clearly tilted towards a YMAG-like large-cap bias. HHIS:CA may still do well because of its leverage and more open upside setup, but the underlying portfolio leaves room for ifs and buts. That room could have been answered by empirical performance proofs, which is not sufficient at this point.

Performance

With whatever performance data we have, I have compared HHIS:CA's total returns with QQCC:CA, QQCL:CA, and even QYLD, since all these ETFs are heavily NDX like in composition. HHIS:CA's far higher total returns are too extraordinary to be ignored, even if the tenure is short. It has expectedly outshined the rest, including a leveraged QQCL:CA, in the rebound post the tariff sell-off in April 2025. This level of outperformance cannot be ignored.

I checked the drawdown performance during the tariff sell-off and saw HHIS:CA down by ~28%, ahead of a leveraged QQCL:CA's correction by ~24%. However, given its far superior upside capture, this level of underperformance in correcting markets should be deemed acceptable.

Yields and Expenses

The headline management fees are zero, but the underlying ETFs' expenses are passed on to HHIS:CA investors. Even the component ETFs like the Harvest NVIDIA Enhanced High Income Shares ETF report only a 0.4% fee, but when trading costs and operating expenses are added, total costs are fairly expected to be above 1% for all the component ETFs. HHIS:CA's expenses should therefore be assumed to be between 1-1.5% in reality - high but expected for an actively managed and leveraged ETF. The expense ratio also looks justified given the significant performance superiority so far.

The fixed dividend payout of $0.25 monthly so far implies a yield of ~24% on the initial January price of ~$12.38 and ~23% at today's levels. I would categorize these yield levels as moderately aggressive. When compared to some YieldMax products that have even higher yields, HHIS:CA's yields do not look as concerning. Given the leverage and return enhancement advantages, HHIS:CA may well manage to keep NAV intact while paying out the high dividends. That story may be challenged in prolonged downturns, but is not as concerning. As long as leverage and the option layer generate sufficient income and capital appreciation is also on track, NAV erosion is not a systematic threat, as seen in several other option income products.

Final Call

Overall, I find HHIS:CA a cautious buy. It ticks all the boxes, from a lower level of option writing capped at 50% that allows the capital to grow, to a leverage mechanism that should suit a growth-minded investor. In the long run, assuming US tech remains bullish, HHIS:CA will continue to do well. Even if the scenario changes, active management has a chance to intervene and align the portfolio to any changing macro environment, unlike a passive methodology that will often wait out a large part of such regime changes to play out and react only after looking backwards.

The only reason I label HHIS:CA as a "cautious" buy is because its active management is heavily tested by a tenure spanning various market cycles. Plus, the underlying concentration on large cap looks theoretically not as well aligned to capture the benefits of a more balanced YMAX like structure. However, even then, the 8-9 months of performance data has fortunately gone through a decent crash and rally phase and shows significant outperformance to take an early bet.

An option writing income plan can be a buy either if it improves the volatility profile against the alternative of directly owning the stocks its writes options on, or if it provides higher growth potential. HHIS:CA falls in the latter category. It is not for a conservative income investor. More growth oriented investors may find its high-risk higher-returns mechanism more digestible. Buy HHIS:CA for its total return promise over volatility management - in the case of income ETFs, often the best defense is a good offense!

data.title: Rethinking U.S. Equity Exposure: A New Index Approach
data.description:
By Jason Anderlik

With global fragmentation accelerating and geopolitical risks rising, the traditional assumptions underpinning U.S. equity investing may be due for a fresh look. While the S&P 500® has long served as the cornerstone for broad-based U.S. market exposure, globalization has reshaped the revenue profile of many constituents. Today, more than a quarter of the companies in the index generate the majority of their revenue outside the U.S.1

In response, S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC (S&P DJI) has licensed to Global X two newly launched innovative benchmarks that aim to refine how S&P DJI measures U.S. equity large-cap companies: the S&P 500 U.S. Revenue Leaders Index and the S&P 500 U.S. Revenue Market Leaders 50 Index.

Reassessing Traditional Market Exposure

Globalization was once a tailwind. But increasingly, foreign revenue may introduce hidden risks that can complicate a U.S.-focused investment thesis. Companies with significant overseas exposure may face regulatory uncertainties, operational inefficiencies and currency translation losses that don’t always show up in domicile-based analyses. Moreover, in an environment of rising tariffs and shifting trade alliances, these risks are potentially more realizable.

This factor is especially important today, as the U.S. continues to outpace peer economies. Between 2019 and 2024, the U.S. economy grew faster than every other G7 country, driven by a surge in technology investments, infrastructure spending and resilient consumer demand. Investors seeking to align portfolios with this growth may benefit from a more targeted lens.

Two Indices, One Goal: A Stronger U.S. Core

In response to this development, S&P DJI constructed indices that emphasize domestic economic alignment over global reach and licensed those indices to Global X for use with exchange-traded funds.

  • The S&P 500 U.S. Revenue Leaders Index selects companies from the S&P 500 that derive the majority of their revenue from within the U.S. The result is a purer proxy for U.S. economic exposure that reduces the impact of foreign revenue streams.
  • The S&P 500 U.S. Revenue Market Leaders 50 Index goes a step further. It filters for companies that not only have at least 50% U.S. revenue but also rank highly on a Market Leader Score. This score is based on sustained free cash flow margins, return on invested capital and market share. These three metrics are closely linked to quality and operational resilience, and they provide a factor-based approach to pure-play U.S. equity indexing.

Conclusion

The world of 2025 looks very different from the one in which the S&P 500 was incepted. In this new landscape, the licensing relationship between Global X and S&P DJI offers a timely new measure of U.S. equity investing. This approach recognizes the evolving realities of global markets and the potential strategic value of domestic focus.

These indices aren’t just alternatives; they represent a new framework for investors who want to be precise about what “U.S. exposure” means.

The author would like to thank Scott Helfstein for his contributions to this blog.

1FactSet Research Systems. (n.d.). S&P 500 [United States Revenue Percentage]. Data accessed July 7th, 2025.

Disclosures:

Information provided by Global X Management Company LLC.

Investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal. Diversification does not ensure a profit nor guarantee against a loss.

The Indexes’ focus on companies that earn the majority of their revenue in the United States may increase sector concentration and reduce exposure to internationally diversified firms, which could cause the Indexes to underperform the broader S&P 500 when foreign-revenue companies outperform. In addition, the Market Leader Score is derived from historical quantitative factors (free cash-flow margins, return on invested capital and market share) that may not predict future performance and can lead to higher turnover and associated costs.

data.title: Is JEPI Silently Eroding Your Wealth?
data.description:
I first analyzed the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (NYSEARCA:JEPI) in December 2024, concluding that investing in the fund, both in the near term and over the long haul, would be an unwise decision. Was I wrong in my assessment?

In this thesis, I’ll revisit that call, in addition to revealing how investing in JEPI can quietly erode investors’ wealth, largely due to the subdued performance of its covered call strategy.

Finally, I’ll outline my future outlook for the fund and explain why I’ve upgraded my rating from a “strong sell” to a “hold.”

A Quick Primer

Note: This section is a general introduction to JEPI. If you’re already familiar with the ETF, feel free to skip to the next section.

According to the fund’s webpage:

The JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPI) seeks to deliver monthly distributable income and equity market exposure with reduced volatility.

JEPI achieves this objective by splitting its capital between two sleeves: equity-linked notes (ELNs) and equities that are primarily, though not exclusively, drawn from the S&P 500.

The ELN sleeve functions much like a covered call strategy, generating income through premiums, which in turn purportedly supports the fund’s monthly distributions. (Readers who are interested in learning more about JEPI’s ELN strategy can read my articles here and here.)

As of August 31, 2025, the fund reported a 30-day SEC yield of 7.27%, an expense ratio of 0.35%, and $41.27 billion in assets under management.

Was I Wrong?

Let’s begin this thesis by answering the question: was I wrong in advising investors to use their shares of broader market indices as a source of income, rather than relying on JEPI? Long story short, I was not.

Since my thesis on this fund on December 18, 2025, JEPI has underperformed SPY by 8.96% on a total return basis.

Expanding the comparison to 1-year and 5-year periods makes the picture even bleaker, with SPY outperforming JEPI by about 14% and 40%, respectively. To put that in perspective, a 40% forgone return over five years equates to an investment with a CAGR of just 7%. (Please refer to JEPI's page on Morningstar to verify those numbers).

Proponents of JEPI may argue that the fund witnessed smaller drawdowns in March following Trump’s tariff announcements. Yet, that achievement looks far less impressive when viewed through the lens of risk-adjusted returns.

Specifically, JEPI has a Sharpe ratio of 0.55, compared to SPY’s 0.95, highlighting that its ability to generate returns per unit of risk is materially weaker than the broader market index.

An Experiment

In my previous thesis on JEPI, I revealed that although management generates sufficient income through the ELN sleeve, it also incurs significant realized losses on its assets. That trend continued in 2025.

For the year ending June 30, 2025, JEPI earned $3,125,621,900 in income but recorded $1,139,569,105 in realized equity losses, resulting in gross income of $1,986,052,795 (page 54 of the annual financial statements).

Since its distributions totaled $3,027,394,985, the shortfall of $2,828,789,706 could only have come from unrealized capital gains. While JEPI may rightly and legally report this as income on its Section 19 notices, from an accounting perspective it is more akin to a return of capital and/or long-term capital gains distribution.

The income-to-loss ratio, however, improved in 2025 to 2.74 ($3,125,621,900 ÷ $1,139,569,105), compared to 0.98 ($2,326,206,734 ÷ $2,351,288,323) in 2024. I’ll provide probable reasons for this improvement in a later section.

For this section, though, the key takeaway from this number crunching is that using JEPI for income is essentially equivalent to selling shares for income. Therefore, comparing JEPI to a broader market index, under the assumption that shares will be sold for income, is not only reasonable but necessary.

With that in mind, let’s compare JEPI’s performance against SPY and QQQ, assuming investors withdrew capital gains from those indices. To make the test even more stringent (and unfair) for the indices, I’ll compare their results to JEPI’s total returns (with dividends reinvested and no withdrawals).

Using data from Yahoo! Finance, I simulated an initial investment of $100,000 with monthly withdrawals.

Between September 2020 and September 2025, JEPI produced an ending value of $168,379 on a total return basis. For SPY, an investor withdrawing capital gains at an annualized rate of 4.9% (spread over 12 months) would have ended with $169,167. For QQQ, the breakeven withdrawal rate was higher at 5.8%, producing a final portfolio value of $168,513.

To summarize, those 4.9% and 5.9% yields essentially represent “free income” for investors choosing SPY or QQQ over JEPI. Put differently, investors in JEPI are quietly and unknowingly eroding their wealth relative to those who have invested in broader market indices when viewed through the lens of opportunity cost.

It’s also worth noting that the simulations above do not account for investor discipline. For example, those who avoid withdrawals during sharp drawdowns, such as the one in March after the tariff announcement, would achieve significantly higher total returns over the long term.

Important Note

Total return figures on Yahoo! Finance differ slightly from those reported by Morningstar. I consider Morningstar’s numbers more reliable, given its long track record as a profitable, established financial data provider.

That said, the discrepancies are small enough that the breakeven withdrawal rates derived above would remain broadly accurate. In numerical terms, even with precise numbers, breakeven withdrawals would likely fall between 4.5% and 5.5% for SPY and 5.5% and 6.5% for QQQ.

The Culprit

In my view, the primary reason for JEPI’s lackluster performance is its inefficient options strategy, which forces the fund to sell equities at unfavorable prices.

When short call options finish in the money, JEPI must record losses on its assets - a hypothesis that becomes more credible when one digs deeper.

JEPI sells weekly options with a delta of about 0.3 (30-delta). This means the ELN counterparty writes options with a 30% chance of finishing in the money. (If you’re unfamiliar with the connection between delta and probability of finishing ITM, see this explanation.)

At first glance, a 70% probability of finishing out of the money looks attractive. But the devil’s in the details.

As of September 15, 2025, SPY options expiring on September 22, 2025 (one week later) had a delta of 0.31973 at the $665 strike.

With SPY trading at $660 on September 15, that left only a ~0.75% buffer. Weekly options are appropriate for this analysis since JEPI’s management structures monthly exposure into weekly buckets.

I reviewed how often SPY delivered weekly returns greater than 0%, 0.5%, and 1% for the years ending June 2024 and June 2025.

For 2024, SPY returned more than 1% in 21 weeks; in 2025, it did so in 18 weeks. These figures suggest JEPI’s options sleeve finished ITM in each of those weeks, meaning the fund likely incurred realized losses on its positions. Note that more weeks of losses also means fewer weeks of gains.

The broader point is this: while all covered call funds face capped upside, JEPI’s oversimplified approach exacerbates the problem by amplifying realized losses.

Why the Rating Upgrade?

I recently wrote an article titled Will Powell Hint At 100 Bps Cuts And Ignite A Bull Run? where I concluded that the Fed is in a comfortable position to cut rates, which should increase the supply of goods and services.

That added supply would be absorbed by resilient demand, leading to stronger revenues and profits for U.S. companies- without sparking additional inflation.

A gradual reduction in interest rates, paired with steady GDP growth, is the “soft landing” the Fed has been talking about since interest rate hikes. Such an environment could also bring subdued volatility.

Lower volatility and lower interest rates will have both positive and negative consequences for JEPI’s income.

Option premiums will shrink. In weeks when JEPI collects premiums, they will be smaller than those earned during the higher-rate years. (Readers interested in the connection between interest rates and option premiums can learn more here.)

Lower volatility also reduces the probability of options finishing in the money at a given strike. With less risk priced in, options demand, and therefore option premiums, will shrink further and cut into JEPI’s income.

For instance, weekly returns were more volatile in 2025 (standard deviation of 2.57%) than in 2024 (1.63%), which helps explain why the income-to-loss ratio that I alluded to earlier improved in 2025 relative to 2024.

On the flip side, a lower-volatility environment pushes 30-delta options further out of the money. For example, in the scenario discussed in the section titled The Culprit, a 30-delta option sat at a $665 strike with SPY at $660. In a lower-volatility setting, the same delta could appear closer to a $630 strike, creating a much wider buffer for JEPI’s options positions. This environment could reduce realized losses.

JEPI does have another saving grace: it has not experienced NAV degradation since inception. In other words, while capital has been employed to fund distributions, the fund has managed to preserve its NAV.

Conclusion

I expect JEPI to reduce losses on its options sleeve in the coming years, with both total returns and NAV trending upward. However, the fund will still underperform broad market indices, and investors will continue to erode wealth in terms of opportunity cost.

Thus, while JEPI is no longer a “strong sell,” it isn’t a buy either, leading me to issue a “hold” rating.

data.description:
Quarterly Highlights

  • The Victory RS Mid Cap Growth Fund (Class A Shares, without sales charge) returned 18.82% for the three months ended June 30, 2025, outperforming the Russell Midcap® Growth Index,[1] which returned 18.20%.
  • Fund performance relative to the benchmark was positively impacted by stock selection in the Producer Durables sector; outperformance was partially offset by stock selection in the Consumer Discretionary sector.
  • This period’s absolute performance was aided in part by the relative performance of secular mid-cap growth stocks as mid growth, as defined by the Russell Midcap® Growth Index, outperformed mid-cap value, as defined by the Russell Midcap® Value Index,[2] 18.20% vs. 5.35%.
  • We continue to believe technology-oriented companies are better positioned for the longer term, especially following the massive “test run” of technology-aided solutions people have leveraged to work, shop, and communicate from home, which we believe has permanently sped up the adoption of these new technologies at the expense of legacy products and services.
  • Mid-cap growth stocks have outperformed their mid-cap value counterparts over 3, 10 and 15 years as of June 30, 2025, per Russell.

Market Performance / Fundamentals Snapshot

Value = Russell 2000® Value Index

Value = Russell Midcap® Value Index

Value = Russell 1000® Value Index

Core = Russell 2000® Index

Growth = Russell 2000® Growth Index

Index returns are for illustrative purposes only and do not represent actual Fund performance. Index performance does not reflect management fees, transaction costs or expenses. Indexes are unmanaged and one cannot invest directly in an index. Past performance does not guarantee future results.

Market Commentary

The second quarter of 2025 was marked by extreme volatility, sentiment swings and uncertainty. The market spiraled lower during the first part of April, a continuation and acceleration of the tariff-induced sell-off that characterized the prior quarter. But virtually overnight, sentiment flipped and markets began a V-shaped recovery. The S&P 500® Index,[3] the proxy for the overall domestic stock market, ultimately logged new all-time highs and delivered an impressive return of 10.9% for the second quarter. The tech-heavy Nasdaq fared even better, rising by 18.0% during the quarter.

The catalyst for this abrupt turnaround was, not surprisingly, also tariff related. The Liberation Day tariff announcement shock in early April that caused market turmoil was quickly replaced with optimism that more trade-friendly tariff agreements would soon be negotiated. The markets, and especially beaten-down growth stocks, moved off the worst-case scenario and staged a remarkable rebound.

During the second quarter, many of the growth market leaders—with a few notable exceptions—grabbed the limelight again. Information Technology and Communication Services stocks, which were laggards earlier in the year due in part to high valuations and worries about supply chains, once again led the market upward. Industrials, traditionally not a growth-centric sector, also raced higher due in part to optimism about infrastructure spending, onshoring and reshoring. Meanwhile, some of the more traditional value-oriented sectors—such as Energy, Health Care and Basic Materials—lagged the broader market.

In terms of investment styles, growth stocks outperformed their value counterparts by a wide margin during the second quarter. Tariff uncertainties and recession concerns abated, as investors looked through the noise and uncertainty. The Russell 3000® Growth Index[4] increased an impressive 17.6% during the second quarter versus a more modest 3.8% rise for the Russell 3000® Value Index.5 Mid-cap and large-cap growth stocks were the clear leaders. The Russell Midcap® Growth Index and the Russell 1000® Growth Index[6] increased by 18.2% and 17.8%, respectively, during the quarter. Perhaps investors wanted to take advantage of pullbacks in market leaders that occurred earlier this year. Small-cap growth stocks, represented by the Russell 2000® Growth Index,[7] also enjoyed a strong second quarter with a 12.0% rebound.

Although we are pleased with the abrupt change in sentiment and strong second quarter market rebound, we implore investors to maintain an even keel and take a longer-term perspective. Recession fears may have abated for the moment, but we still do not have clarity on the new administration’s trade policies and the impact that any tariffs ultimately may have on earnings. More episodes of volatility are likely as we continue living in uncertain times.

For example, questions remain regarding monetary policy. Recent statements by the Federal Reserve suggest more willingness to lower rates later this year, but the Board of Governors remain data-dependent, as the lasting effects of tariffs on inflation and employment remain uncertain. And, unfortunately, the geopolitical hotspots around the globe remain in place, and U.S.–China relations are tenuous at best.

But as we’ve discussed in the past, market tumult and volatility also breed opportunity. We continue to believe that the current environment is an attractive backdrop for active managers in the growth space, particularly those with a long time horizon and a commitment to a vetted investment philosophy. So while there is no definitive clarity on several possible market-moving variables, there are still many innovative, growth-oriented companies across various sectors of the economy, in our opinion. Our team continues to focus on identifying potential opportunities within secular growth, and we believe that we are still in the early stages of a secular shift that is transforming how consumers, businesses, and employees interact. That trend, along with new emerging technologies (including artificial intelligence), is poised to have a significant impact in the years ahead, particularly among growth-oriented investment styles.

Investment Strategy

The Victory RS Mid Cap Growth Fund (the “Fund”) is guided by our philosophy that sustainable earnings growth drives long-term share price appreciation. Our investment process is focused on finding innovative companies whose core business, we believe, can grow from a mid-capitalization company to a large-cap company over time.

The team seeks companies with products and services that are growing organically, creating new markets or taking market share from existing companies. We are focused on finding companies whose business values can appreciate regardless of the underlying market environment. The Strategy is led by the team’s chief investment officer, Scott Tracy, along with portfolio managers Steve Bishop, Melissa Chadwick-Dunn, Paul Leung, and Trevor Martin. The five co-portfolio managers, as well as two research analysts, serve as sector specialists and are supported by two associates, drawing on strong relationships with industry experts and company management teams.

Together, we conduct over 2,000 company meetings each year through in-person meetings, conference calls, and trade shows. We then back up our findings through discussions with industry leaders and third-party sources. We are long-term investors and seek to establish definable “anchor points,” which are quantifiable metrics that help determine a company’s potential long-term growth trajectory. Anchor points arise from our analysis of a company’s long-term capabilities and performance goals over three to five years. These long-term anchor points serve as guideposts to help us measure a company’s progress as it executes its business strategy, regardless of what is taking place in the overall market, and help prevent distractions caused by short-term stock price movements and inevitable market volatility.

Performance Review

The Victory RS Mid Cap Growth Fund (Class A Shares, without sales charge) returned 18.82% for the three months ended June 30, 2025, outperforming the Russell Midcap® Growth Index, which returned 18.20%. Fund performance relative to the benchmark was positively impacted by stock selection in the Producer Durables sector; outperformance was partially offset by stock selection in the Consumer Discretionary sector. This period’s absolute performance was aided in part by the relative performance of secular mid-cap growth stocks as mid growth, as defined by the Russell Midcap® Growth Index, outperformed mid-cap value, as defined by the Russell Midcap® Value Index, 18.20% vs. 5.35%. We continue to believe technology-oriented companies are better positioned for the longer term, especially following the massive “test run” of technology-aided solutions people have leveraged to work, shop, and communicate from home, which we believe has permanently sped up the adoption of these new technologies at the expense of legacy products and services. Mid-cap growth stocks have outperformed their mid-cap value counterparts over 3, 10 and 15 years as of June 30, 2025, per Russell.

Top Contributing Sector: Producer Durables

One of the largest drivers of relative outperformance within the Producer Durables sector was Curtiss-Wright Corporation (CW) (0.56% ending weight), an aerospace & defense company levered to multiple high-growth military and space programs, as well as a burgeoning commercial nuclear technology business benefiting from the expansion of small nuclear reactors (SMR). Curtiss-Wright is a leading player in many of the markets it serves and currently is positioned well for long-term secular growth. We continue to hold the position.

Top Detracting Sector: Consumer Discretionary

One of the larger areas of underperformance within the Consumer Discretionary sector was driven by affordable homebuilding company Champion Homes, Inc. (SKY) (0.39% ending weight). Champion had a difficult first quarter of results driven by poor weather and a hesitancy by the consumer in the short term to purchase higher-margin add-on products from their product lineup. This more discretionary spending was affected by stubbornly high interest rates, which we expect to be mitigated in the coming years. We continue to like the long-term prospects for further market share gains at Champion and continue to hold the position.

Market and Strategy Outlook

We are confident that the market has stabilized in the second quarter and are hopeful that earnings will begin to drive stocks versus external factors such as tariffs, monetary policy and regulatory regime changes. U.S. employment remains strong, and the outlook for solid earnings growth in 2025 remains in place. While the full impact on the economy and employment from the recently announced tariffs is yet unclear, a more normal, steady economic state should be a very favorable backdrop for growth stocks. In addition, the potential for more robust domestic spending in the areas of onshoring and reshoring could make the U.S. a relative safe haven. Certain segments of the growth economy appear well positioned to continue to meaningfully outgrow overall GDP, while company valuations are increasingly compelling for long-term investors.

In the current environment, there continues to be a unique opportunity for secular growth companies outside of the mega-cap growth category. Despite their relative underperformance since November 2020, smaller-cap secular growth companies have displayed continued strength in their underlying fundamentals. We expect this fundamental strength to persist and are optimistic that the valuation gap of smaller companies will narrow substantially with that of their larger-cap peers.

Overall, while we believe the tariff picture is slowly getting somewhat clearer, the engineering of a soft landing for the economy remains unclear. Investors are increasingly looking through toward 2026 and beyond, extending market gains. As we navigate the market at or near old highs, we think there will be bouts of volatility and we are ready to take advantage of dislocations in company valuations. As such, we are patient and believe that opportunities will emerge in sectors and industries that demonstrate secular resilience through the cycle and sustained growth. We feel that secular growth companies within the growth universe are especially attractive relative to cyclicals due to their strong and consistent fundamentals and potential for outperformance.

Thank you for your continued investment.

data.title: Stride: The Hidden Educational Infrastructure Play Trading At A GARP Sweet Spot
data.description:
When Stride (NYSE:LRN) crossed pandemic highs and set up another all-time high above $170, the market had fundamentally misunderstood the structural transformation of this education technology giant. As investors focus on post-COVID normalization and K-12 enrollment volatility, they are missing the more important narrative: the "Educational Infrastructure Transition." In simple words, with steady pricing at pre-pandemic levels, educational institutions are now adopting Stride's technology backbone, which can trigger a recurring revenue stream with sustainable pricing power and market expansion over the next 3-5 years, supported by 80%+ gross margins.

This isn't just another online education recovery story. Stride is undergoing a fundamental change in the way education is delivered across America. From its K-12 virtual schools to its Career Learning division seeing a 35% growth year over year, workforce programs are rapidly evolving. Stride is the very definition of Growth at a Reasonable Price with its PEG ratio at 0.76, forward P/E at 17.3x, and EBITDA growth at 28.6%. The technical setup supports this thesis, with the stock consolidating at critical resistance levels around the $160 mark after nearly a decade of strong gains. Stride is a Buy with a 12-month price target of $210.

Business Update: The Numbers That Paint the Picture

The company achieved an unprecedented record year of strong business performance. Revenue increased to $2.41 billion, an 18 percent increase from the previous year. In FY2024, revenue reached $2.04 billion. Adjusted EPS rose to nearly $8.10, significantly higher compared to $4 the prior year, an increase of nearly 75%. This shows the company's ability to turn cost changes into profits.

The segment's results show that the true transformation is happening now. Career Learning revenue enjoyed an annual growth rate of 35%, reaching $876.3 million within the year and making it Stride's most profitable division. CEO James Rhyu noted during the latest earnings call that their Career Learning segment is continually outpacing the rest of the company. He noted that the need for skills training, which the company's platform is uniquely positioned to provide, is also growing.

The K-12 virtual schooling initiative produced nearly $1.45 billion in revenue while also reducing the per-unit cost for parents. Student enrollment has stabilized at roughly 234,000 despite pandemic-related disruptions, while revenue per student has increased by 12 percent, driven by adding a wide variety of classes and technology services for other districts.

The year before, the company reported $279 million, compared to $433 million now. The retail industry's financial sector averaged about $8.66 per share. The company performed well above the sector average, supporting future investments and expansion. EBITDA margin increased to 23.7%, up from 14.5% over the last three years, showing that the company's new model is highly effective.

The Educational Infrastructure Transition: The Thesis Wall Street is Missing

While the market remains fixated on enrollment numbers and state funding dynamics, it is overlooking the more fundamental structural shift that I call the Educational Infrastructure Transition. Just as cloud computing revolutionized enterprise IT through the provision of scalable and efficient alternatives to on-premises systems, Stride is becoming the infrastructure backbone for educational institutions seeking to modernize their delivery capabilities.

This transition unfolds in three ways that create sustainable competitive advantages. First, Technology Platform Monetization: Stride's proprietary learning management system, curriculum development tools, and student information systems are increasingly being licensed to traditional school districts seeking hybrid learning capabilities. An 80 percent gross margin is well above the 60 to 65 percent margins typically earned on more traditional enrollment-based revenue.

Additionally, the Skills-Based Learning Revolution in the Career Learning division is not only growing; it's seizing the huge secular trend toward competency-based education desired by corporate America. Underpinned by this $30 billion+ market opportunity, we offer Fortune 500 multi-year, sticky contracts. These contracts are largely recession resistant.

In addition, the introduction of state laws is clearing away regulatory roadblocks from virtual and hybrid learning. The Every Student Succeeds Act (ESSA) gives states more authority to expand educational options. Since 2020, 35 states have passed legislation to allow for expanded virtual school access, thereby creating a regulatory environment that favors Stride over new entrants.

The transition of infrastructure is already creating pricing power. Stride has raised tuition 4-6% each year across its portfolio while simultaneously expanding margins through operating leverage. The partner districts of the company have a Net Promoter Score of 67, which indicates a low churn risk.

Competitive Analysis: The Moat That Competitors Cannot Cross

The online education market has grown significantly with the arrival of new players, notably Pearson, Grand Canyon Education (LOPE), and several small regional players. Stride's other structural advantages create a competitive moat around it.

Stride has invested over $1 billion in curriculum development over the past nine years. They now have content libraries in more than 300 courses in K-12 and career training. Rivals cannot economically match this investment since the content must be regularly updated to ensure compliance with state standards.

As more students enroll, Stride can feed their data into its AI personalization engine. With 180,000+ active students generating millions of learning interactions annually, Stride's AI personalization algorithms will become increasingly effective. This creates a virtuous cycle, where better outcomes attract more students and partners, which in turn generates even better data.

Stride has regulatory expertise in state education laws across more than 20 states with varying funding formulas, special education requirements, and accountability standards. Regulatory knowledge of education represents a significant barrier to entry for tech companies that want to operate in the education sector.

Competitive positioning is even stronger in Career Learning, where Stride competes mainly against local community colleges and corporate training providers. Traditional institutions lack the technology infrastructure to deliver personalized, competency-based training at scale. Meanwhile, pure-play training companies lack the academic credentialing capabilities that employers increasingly demand.

Financial Analysis: Margin Expansion and Cash Generation Excellence

Stride's years of transformation have proven that an asset-light business model is possible with hard work. Stride significantly improved its EBITDA this year, reaching a 23.7% margin, compared to 14.5% in its first year, while maintaining a rapid growth rate. Overall corporate margins have been rising, partly due to the strong reputation companies have for delivering valuable and profitable training.

In 2025, ROIC rose 55 percent over the past period. Numerous staffing firms are able to generate substantial profits without heavy capital expenditures in this economic downfall that the country is currently facing.

The company can make its own choices about how to spend its $985 million. The company was able to make additional strategic moves thanks to $435 billion in improvements like abundant money in the bank.

Stride working capital has doubled, supporting the company's shift to a subscription service. Shorter sales cycles are generating more predictable income.

Valuation: Compressed Multiples Despite Structural Transformation

According to Stride Inc.'s Q1 fiscal 2025 earnings release filed on October 22, 2024, Stride reported impressive EPS growth of 755% year-over-year to $0.94, significantly exceeding analyst expectations of $0.78. Margins for the technology company also expanded, yet Stride trades at a forward P/E of only 17.3x after the announcement. This P/E valuation is substantially discounted relative to both Stride's technology peers and its own historic multiples. The market has failed to fully appreciate the quality and sustainability of Stride's business transformation, creating a substantial upside.

Based on prudent assumptions reflecting the structural transformation of Stride's business model, I have modelled my DCF. We expect a revenue CAGR of 12% till 2028 alongside an EBITDA margin of 23-24%, backed by Career Learning expansion. Further, strong FCF owing to asset-light operations. My DCF analysis provides the following projections based on these expectations.

Year Revenue ($M) EBITDA ($M) EBITDA Margin Free Cash Flow ($M)
2025 2,405 570 23.7% 485
2026 2,766 656 23.7% 558
2027 3,126 741 23.7% 630
2028 3,501 830 23.7% 705
2029 3,851 913 23.7% 776
  • Terminal Value: $12,217M
  • Enterprise Value: $10,216M
  • Fair Value: $234.86 per share

The market expects Stride to maintain a WACC of 9%, reflecting their strong balance sheet and predictable cash flows from contracted enrollment revenue. The company is expected to enjoy a terminal growth of 3%. The EBITDA margins are expected to remain stable at 23.7% with operational leverage from technology platform scaling, maintaining the conservative 23.7% margin recently achieved in fiscal 2025.

The peer analysis further supports upside potential. Grand Canyon Education trades at 22x forward earnings despite slower growth. Adtalem Global Education trades at 20x, reflecting its inferior margins. Applying my 2026 EPS estimate of $11.20, with a conservative multiple of 21x, it results in a fair value of $235, consistent with my DCF analysis.

Stride's valuation opportunity is confirmed by its Seeking Alpha's Quant Rating system, which rates the stock as a Buy, with strong Growth (B), Profitability (A), Momentum (A), and Revisions (A+) scores. An attractive thesis is supported by a D+ valuation grade, suggesting the stock is attractively priced despite high quality.

Technical Analysis: Confirming the Fundamental Breakout

The technical setup strongly supports the thesis of a fundamental transformation. Stride has maintained a long-term uptrend since 2016. The return posted is over 2,000%. This is a great example of secular growth trends in education technology.

The way price is currently consolidating is a healthy sign after the gap-up of over 10% in August. Institutional recognition of the business model change helped close this gap during the record fiscal year. The stock's strength above these levels, despite market weakness, indicates there is strong demand for the stock.

The weekly chart shows that the 50-, 100-, and 200-day moving averages are all trending upwards and supporting prices. The stochastic oscillator is down from overbought levels, leaving room for a higher leg of the trend. Stride typically consolidate for 2-3 months after every surge in the primary uptrend.

Keep an eye on these resistance levels at $175 (previous all-time high) and $190 (upper channel resistance). If it clears $175 with volume, our first target is $210 after 6-9 months.

Catalysts for the Next 12 Months

There are many catalysts lined up, particularly to support the stock's performance over the next 12 months. There is a time for each one, and their effect can be measured. This reinforces the investment thesis.

Q1 Fiscal 2026 Results (Oct 28, 2025): This represents the most important near-term catalyst. Career Learning is expected to accelerate sequentially, with revenue growth above 35% year-over-year, reaching $240 million for the quarter. New corporate partnerships announced in Q1 are particularly relevant, as they help validate the $30 billion+ TAM thesis. Every major tie-up with a Fortune 500 company typically generates $10-15 million annually, with EBITDA margins of 25% or more. Management stated that 8-12 new partnerships are in advanced negotiations, potentially adding $80-150 million in contracted revenue.

Stride's B2B platform licensing is expected to generate first meaningful revenue in Q2 FY2026. The firm has partnered with over 25 school districts, encompassing more than 150,000 students. The full launch would generate approximately $750-900 per student annually in licensing fees, equating to $110-135 million in incremental, high-margin revenue. This new revenue stream could lead to a $200+ million annual run rate by fiscal year 2027. Federal Education Funding Allocation (November 2025): The Department of Education's Title I funding formula updates. The anticipated updates in November could strongly favor virtual school operators. Early signs point to a bump in per-pupil funding worth 6-8 percent nationally, while states like Texas and Florida may see double-digit initiatives. Each 1% funding increase would translate to $12-15 million in additional revenue from Stride's 180,000 enrolled students, with over 70% EBITDA flow-through.

Completion of strategic acquisition (Q2-Q3 FY2026): Management is evaluating acquisition opportunities within the adult education and professional certification sectors. With $435 million in net cash and over $300 million in debt capacity, Stride could strategically deploy up to $500 million. Companies in the corporate training space generally trade at revenue multiples of 3-5x. As a result, through acquisitions, Stride could potentially acquire companies with revenues of $100-150 million. Further, acquiring such firms would immediately accelerate Career Learning growth.

Earnings Guidance Revision (Multiple Quarters): I believe management will revise fiscal 2026 guidance upward by Q2, on the improving trend in Career Learning and platform licensing. Given the growth rate of the high-margin business, the current estimate of $2.68 billion in revenue and a $10.20 EPS appears conservative. If guidance is revised to revenues exceeding $2.75 billion and earnings over $11.00, a significant multiple expansion would be expected.

Risk Factors That Could Impact the Thesis

I think the bullish thesis is exposed to four main risks, which I keep a close watch on. Each risk has a clear trigger for reassessing my positions. The biggest danger to success is negative changes or interpretations in laws or regulations at various levels.

Now, states such as California and New York are already considering legislation to place a 5 percent cap on the growth of virtual school enrollment. If any such measures were enforced on Stride, they could impact over 35,000 students, or almost 20% of Stride's enrollment, and annual revenue of $180-220 million. A new federal government administration could change the Title I funding formulas for the worse. I check this risk quarterly through tracking reports on state legislation and Department of Education policy announcements. A 10% drop in same-state enrollment for two straight quarters would mean a thesis reassessment.

Market saturation and competition in corporate training appear likely, and there may be many other giant players like Microsoft, Google, and Amazon entering with competition platforms to reduce growth rates significantly. These businesses are better funded and have developed relationships with companies, which may undercut Stride. There are some signs indicating a recession threat, like non-accomplishment of large corporate training contracts, margin pressure at the Career Learning segment, and declining win rates. I review quarterly disclosures of contracts and management commentary for more clarity on competition. A reduced growth of Career Learning below 20% in two successive quarters will mean increased competition.

Impact of Economic Recession on Corporate Training Budgets. Many experts see training as recession-proof, but how many organizations actually follow this? Corporations may limit investments in employee training during a major downturn. Stride is a leading Career Learning company. As a result of the corporate training budgets growing on average 15-25% during the 2008-2009 recession, Stride's Career Learning momentum suffered. I keep an eye on capital expenditure spending trends, unemployment in the primary markets, and Fortune 500 earnings guidance revisions. The signing or delay of any new Career Learning contracts that each exceed $50 million would be an economic headwind.

The open and versatile pace of fintech technology adoption by South African banks creates an excellent prospect with strong opportunities for innovation and market penetration by DFS players. The company's technology backbone is designed and able to handle increasing volumes of students while maintaining performance standards required for institutional clients. Integration issues or platform outages could harm customer relationships and slow growth momentum. I focus on monitoring platform uptime statistics (target >99.5%), student satisfaction scores, and customer churn rates. A Net Promoter Score below 60 or student retention under 85% indicates implementation issues and should be addressed as soon as possible.

Stride currently maintains conservative leverage, but aggressive acquisitions or operational mistakes could weaken the balance sheet. To succeed in the long term, it is crucial that the company maintains investment-grade metrics while funding growth initiatives. I monitor the debt-to-EBITDA target for <2.0x, interest coverage for >8x, and free cash flow conversion >80%. If these credit metrics drop below these target levels, or if free cash flow is negative for two consecutive quarters, the position size should be reduced.

Conclusion

Stride Inc. is a promising GARP play with strong growth and reasonable valuation. The Educational Infrastructure Transition thesis will generate multiple layers of value over the next 3-5 years. Its strong balance sheet and defensible market position provide downside protection.

Stride offers investors a rare combination of quality and value, as is evident in its PEG of 0.76, forward P/E of 17.3x, and accelerating cash flow generation. The technical configuration supports this fundamental strength, with clear catalysts lining up over the next 12 months.

I remain Buy Rated with a 12-month price target of $245, representing 54% upside. In a world of scarce GARP opportunities, Stride offers sustainable growth at a reasonable price, backed by structural transformation in a very large addressable market.

data.title: Victory Trivalent International Small-Cap Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Environment

International small-cap equities posted another solid quarter on improving sentiment as investors continue to diversify into the broader global economy, drawn by compelling valuations and strong underlying fundamentals. International returns were further supported by a 7% drop in the U.S. Dollar Index on weaker U.S. confidence following the announcement of widespread tariffs. For the quarter, the S&P® Developed Ex-U.S. SmallCap Index advanced 17.4%, while the Victory Trivalent International Small-Cap Fund (Class A shares at NAV) outperformed the benchmark. The rally was widespread across equity markets, with all countries in the Index posting positive returns. In Asia, South Korea rose 35.7% and was the best-performing country in the Index as shares rallied following the presidential election amid rising optimism for stimulus measures and structural economic reforms. Israel rebounded 30%, driven by strength in high-tech and a resilient labor market, despite continued conflict in Gaza and heightened tensions with Iran. Portugal extended its strong year-to-date performance, rising 24.1% on the back of solid gains in the banking sector. The United Kingdom rose 22.3% on upbeat economic data, a newly signed trade agreement with the U.S., and monetary easing. On the other end of the spectrum, New Zealand posted a modest 3% gain tempered by expectations of slowing growth and tighter government spending.

All 11 sectors posted gains, with cyclical stocks leading the rally. Information Technology climbed 22.7%, driven by strong performance in semiconductors. Industrials rose 20.6% and were supported by growing commitments to infrastructure and defense spending. Utilities gained 17%, led by strength in renewable energy names. The more defensive Consumer Staples sector advanced 9.7%, lagging the broader Index.

Portfolio Review

Positive security selection was broad-based, with excess returns generated in four of five regions and nine of the eleven economic sectors. From a style perspective, the Fund’s overall exposure to value, business momentum and quality factors contributed to relative performance. Business momentum and quality generated the strongest signals, while value was a headwind. At the sector level, notable outperformance was generated in Industrials, with the largest impact coming from two Korean companies. Defense equipment manufacturer Hyundai Rotem rose on higher deliveries and new order inquiries from Eastern European countries. Also in Korea, power equipment manufacturer Hyosung Heavy Industries advanced on rising export demand for its high-voltage power transformers. Relative performance was also strong in Information Technology, supported by a position in Celestica. The Canadian technology services company was the top contributor after posting better-than-expected results and raising guidance. Spending on AI infrastructure from its hyperscaler clients has thus far proven resilient.

While relative performance was strong in Japan, select holdings in the region detracted from overall performance. Japanese pharmaceutical and consumer product manufacturer Tsumura fell after reporting that costs associated with upgrading its production capacity exceeded guidance due to raw material shortages and increased procurement costs. In Financials, Japanese banks including Yamaguchi Financial Group declined on growing uncertainty regarding the effect U.S. tariffs will have on the creditworthiness of its export-focused loan book. In Materials, Daido Steel declined following the announcement of steel tariffs in the U.S. and a slightly weaker-than-expected full-year report.

Market Outlook

International equities have demonstrated strength year-to-date in the face of ongoing tariff uncertainty. While trade tensions continue to pose a headwind to global growth, the European Central Bank has responded with eight rate cuts this year, bringing its policy rate down to 2% as inflation has eased toward target levels. At the same time, rising government investment in infrastructure and defense spending across Europe and Asia presents a meaningful secular tailwind for international equities. Notably, the EU and Germany have committed to robust infrastructure funding, while NATO members have pledged to raise defense spending to 5% of GDP by 2035, well above the prior 2% guideline. In Asia, Japan’s corporate governance reforms and ongoing value-up initiatives are drawing investor attention, driving a rise in shareholder activism and willingness to pay higher multiples. Meanwhile, investment in artificial intelligence remains a dominant global theme, offering the potential to drive substantial productivity gains across industries. On the risk front, geopolitical instability remains amid ongoing international conflicts and global trade tensions. Investors are closely monitoring tariff negotiations and the growing shift toward bilateral trade agreements, while companies explore reshoring efforts to better navigate an increasingly complex trade environment. Against this backdrop, domestically oriented small-cap equities may offer relative resilience, facing less exposure to global trade risks than their large-cap multinational peers. From a valuation perspective, international small-cap stocks are still trading at lower earnings multiples compared to historical averages, and they remain attractively priced relative to both U.S. and international large-cap stocks. We continue to be guided by our bottom-up analysis and will evaluate upcoming earnings and outlook statements to refine our positioning. We believe there are still opportunities for relative outperformance, particularly within the diverse segment of international small-cap equities.

data.title: Victory Diversified Stock Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Investment Philosophy

The Victory Diversified Stock Fund invests in companies that we believe demonstrate strong earnings growth, a healthy return on invested capital, and positive earnings/price momentum combined with a reasonable valuation over a wide spectrum of market capitalizations. We are confident that this combination of characteristics positions the Fund for strong competitive performance. These are the characteristics that have historically contributed to Munder’s successful long-term record, and we firmly believe they should continue to serve our investors well.

Market Overview

In the second quarter, the Victory Diversified Stock Fund A-Shares (without sales charge) outperformed its S&P 500® Index benchmark by approximately 342 basis points (bps). Stock selection contributed 438 bps to outperformance and was positive in every sector except Energy and Materials, while sector allocation detracted 96 bps primarily due to our overweight in Health Care. The Fund’s average sector allocation during the quarter was close to benchmark weights with the exception of a 414-bps overweight in Health Care and a 234-bps overweight in Financials. The Fund was underweight Technology (226 bps) and had no exposure to Utilities.

Within the S&P 500® Index benchmark, growth outperformed value by 15.9%, led by renewed enthusiasm for AI infrastructure spending from large-cap hyperscalers. The Fund’s style factor exposure to Market Sensitivity was the largest positive contributor to performance, and small-cap exposure was the biggest detractor. Overall, the Fund’s style factor exposures were a modest positive contributor in the quarter.

The Fund’s stock selection was strongest in Industrials and Health Care. Within Industrials, Quanta Power and Federal Signal were the best performers. Quanta Power rallied after posting strong backlog growth and earnings amid continued grid modernization and renewed AI infrastructure spending commitments. Federal Signal alleviated fears of government spending cuts impacting its municipal business by reporting earnings and backlog figures that beat investor expectations. In Health Care, while our holdings mostly performed in line with the sector, minimal exposure to large laggards in managed care and biotech such as United Healthcare (UNH) and Novo Nordisk (NVO) aided performance in the quarter.

Negative stock selection came entirely from Energy and Materials. Within both sectors the weakness was broad-based and correlated with the overall volatility and downward pressure put on global commodities such as oil, gas, and precious metals.

On an individual basis, the top positive active contributing stocks were the aforementioned Quanta Power and Jabil. Jabil surged on general enthusiasm for AI and strong financial results within its Intelligent Infrastructure segment as hyperscalers continued to spend on datacenter equipment. The top negative active contributing stocks were JD.com (JD) and an underweight in NVIDIA (NVDA). JD fell victim to tariff concerns and short-term profitability issues in its most recent quarter from the rollout of its food delivery business. NVIDIA recovered prior-quarter losses as results alleviated investor concerns and the AI trade picked up renewed steam. We have since exited our position in JD and decreased our underweight in NVIDIA.

At quarter-end, the Fund is overweight in the Health Care sector and underweight in Consumer Staples, Technology, and Utilities (no exposure).

Overall, the Fund’s market capitalization is approximately 5% lower than the benchmark on a weighted market-cap basis. The Fund continues to have positive exposure to Earnings Yield, Growth, Market Sensitivity, and Profitability.
